一个小贩正在他的手推车上切下看起来像树干的东西。这是在班加罗尔，我居住的印度南部卡纳塔克邦的城市。核心是乳白色，皮肤是棕橙色。他以外科医生的精确度水平地、成圈地切割，薄得我可以看穿碎片。 “这是根。它可以长到 5 英尺深，重达 300 公斤，”他在回应我的震惊时解释道。他说，它来自邻近的喀拉拉邦，来自“从森林中获取它”的人。他没有看到任何人提取根，但看到了它来自的树。 “这就像一个登山者。它送花。它生长在海边。它在印度南部被称为 Bhoochakara Gadda，在北部被称为 Ram Kand Mool。”根可以这么大吗？尤其是攀援者或藤蔓的根？我还没来得及问谷歌，我的零食就准备好了。用盐、辣椒粉和酸橙调味；我丈夫吃了糖和酸橙。它松脆，多汁，清爽，但没有自己的味道。那天晚上，我用谷歌搜索了 Bhoochakara Gadda。没有太多。维基百科将该植物的学名确定为 Maerua oblongifolia，但没有它的照片。我读到 Maerua oblongifolia 是一种低矮的、木质的、低矮的灌木，见于印度、巴基斯坦、非洲部分地区和沙特阿拉伯，它们的块茎作为零食出售，并在 Siddha 的古老医学系统中用作兴奋剂。研究论文显示了它的叶子和花朵，但没有显示根。所以我决定深入研究。我联系了我所在州的一位木材科学家、一位食品科学教授和一位悉达 (Siddha) 实践者。他们没有看到那棵树。我将搜索转向南部的另一个州，安得拉邦。我打电话给它的部落部门，但没有得到任何线索。然后我给生物多样性委员会主席的信息遭到了沉默。我与一位研究过这棵树的植物学研究员交谈过，才得知他从未亲眼见过它。很快，我就会知道，我无所事事的好奇心实际上是一个谜，几十年来一直困扰着，有时甚至激怒了植物学家。几十年来，这种小吃一直在街头广泛出售——从北部的哈里瓦市到西部的浦那和南部的几个地方——然而，不知何故，似乎没有人知道它是什么。几个月后，当我看到 MS Rathore 博士的论文时，我取得了突破，他于 2011 年在实验室中繁殖了 Maerua oblongifolia。他在拉贾斯坦邦的沙漠州多次看到这棵树。 “但我没有听说或看到有人吃根，”科学家在电话中说，听起来很困惑。
“它的根稀少且不可食用，”他的论文导师、退休的植物学教授 NS Shekhawat 博士补充道。 “生长在干旱地区，哪来那么多水长出大根肥多汁？ [小吃] 不可能是长叶月桂。”两人给我发了这棵树的照片，这与维基百科上的描述不符。照片中的根源是在实验室里生长的，太小了，无法得出任何结论。我在印度植物学会前主席 T Pullaiah 博士旁边讲话。在他 2019 年的《世界药用植物百科全书》中，他将这种小吃与 Ipomoea d igitata 联系起来，这是一种攀缘植物，长出带有黄棕色皮毛的大块根。他一定是看到了根，我想。 “不。这是二手知识。我们是教授，忙于教学和行政工作，因此我们依靠现有文献得出结论，”他解释说。所以我打了更多的电话，写了更多的电子邮件。一个新名字出现了，我所有的研究都作废了。那一年是 1994 年。民族植物学家 Koppula Hemadri 博士正在印度各地“挖根”以确认这种小吃的起源。他用龙舌兰结束了他的搜索。这些多肉植物看起来像芦荟，但可以长到 10 英尺宽和两倍高。有些有一个粗壮的茎，上面有带刺的叶子，比如菠萝。有些看起来没有茎。 “我尝到了龙舌兰的根部，它与根部相连，”现已退休并住在安得拉邦的赫马德里博士说。 “它是淀粉质的，有点像那种小吃。但在那之后我没有追求[领先]。”同年，在同一个州，植物学家 Ali Moulali 博士走得更近：“我告诉一位供应商，如果他透露植物的身份，我会多付他 1,000-2,000 卢比（13-27 美元）。 ” 他犹豫了一下，说道：“这是北奈良的基地。” “这就是当地对龙舌兰制成的纤维的称呼。供应商还透露，它不是根，而是生长在地上的东西。
在同一时期，开花植物分类学家 SR Yadav 博士鼓励他在西部马哈拉施特拉邦的学生仔细研究 Ram Kand，这是当地小吃的名称。他的两个学生——博士。正在研究分子生物学和分类学的 Mansingraj S Nimbalkar 和 Vinod B Shimpale 博士继续为这个谜团提供了最科学的见解。 2010 年，经过长时间的解剖学研究，他们对一片小吃进行了 DNA 条形码编码，发现它与龙舌兰的匹配度达 89%。龙舌兰有多种种类，但实验室测试将其范围缩小到龙舌兰 Sisalana，一种有时用来制作类似龙舌兰酒的植物。不久之后，他们进行了实地考察，拔出了剑麻，却发现了网状的浅根。接下来，他们切掉了它的叶子，它就在那里：数百万印度人从食品车上熟悉的肥厚、白色、水汪汪的树干。他们吃了一片，就像拉姆康德一样无味和松脆。研究结果于次年发表在《当代科学》上。那么为什么对这种小吃的身份还存在疑问呢？ “它是哪种龙舌兰——西萨拉纳龙舌兰、美洲龙舌兰还是其他龙舌兰？” Shimpale 博士说。 “在供应商向我们展示工厂之前，我们无法得出结论。他们将此作为商业秘密，以激发人们对它的好奇心。”是的，供应商所说的有一个模式：它是根；它是药用的；他们从 200 公里外的森林或非洲得到它。他们说印度教神拉姆和他的妻子和兄弟在他们被流放到森林期间以拉姆康德为生，而 Bhoochakara Gadda 是一种生长在地下的甜蜜生物。尝试批量购买他们的股票，他们只会留下几片。试探他们，他们就会推车离开。 “马哈拉施特拉邦的森林官员曾试图监视他们，但徒劳无功，”宁巴尔卡博士回忆道。出于一种预感，我给卡纳塔克邦 GS Yadav 的一位高级森林官员打电话。 “你需要获得许可才能从任何森林中移除或提取任何东西，”他明确地说。但是龙舌兰并不完全适合森林支架。它在印度广泛生长，在路边，铁路沿线，作为围栏。然而，它可能不那么健康。 “龙舌兰含有大量生物碱。如果大量食用，它可能会中毒。也许这就是他们出售薄片的原因，”现已退休的 Yadav 博士警告说。我越来越确信龙舌兰是答案，直到班加罗尔的营养与技术助理教授 Chenna Kesava Reddy Sangati 博士将其称为“不可能”。他对龙舌兰进行了广泛的研究，以从其 Albomarginata 品种中生产酒精饮料。 “我吃过 Bhoochakara Gadda。它的口感更顺滑，咬起来更柔软，而且不是很甜，”他说。 “而这种龙舌兰非常甜、涩、纤维质，而且很难咬。”四月，印度的第二波冠状病毒爆发了。街上的人群变少了，手推车更少了，我第一次见到的小贩也不见了。 “他去了他的村子，”在同一条街上卖西瓜的一个人告诉我。但他传递了供应商的联系电话。
由于担心再次封锁，该供应商已返回北部贾坎德邦。大流行正在扼杀他的生意，他想从我这里知道事情什么时候会恢复正常。我能说什么？我们转移了话题，他轻松地说：“这是一个茎……我全年都在卖……我只在照片和电话中看到过这种植物。”哦！ “你能告诉我你从哪里找到 Bhoochakara Gadda 的人的电话号码吗？”我问。他沉默了。 “请不要问这个。没有人会告诉你任何事情。这就是这项业务的方式，”他告诉我，一分钟都被激怒了。然后他冷静了下来。 “我多年来一直在吃这个。它冷却身体。顾客说这对糖尿病有好处。这不违法，我知道。”在质疑我给他打电话的动机后，他同意看专家们争论的植物照片。 “没有，”他回答。我很伤心，但打电话回来感谢他。但随后他说，“照片 3。那是植物。”这是龙舌兰Sisalana。他也同意了第二张照片，那就是美国龙舌兰。 1号照片上没有字，Maerua oblongifolia。 “他们剪下树叶，在树干上涂上红色，以保持其白色，”他解释道。毕竟，这是一种龙舌兰小吃吗？我将等待最后的决定，因为来自拉贾斯坦邦的另一位植物学家 MS Shekhawat 博士向我保证，在大流行结束后，他将进入田间，连根拔起、切片和品尝每个可能候选者的样本，并且一劳永逸地解开谜团。 Gastro Obscura 涵盖了世界上最美妙的食物和饮料。注册我们的电子邮件，每周发送两次。
note_en:
A vendor was cutting slices off what looked like the trunk of a tree on his push-cart. This was in Bengaluru, the city in the south Indian state of Karnataka where I live. The core was a creamy white and the skin a brownish-orange. He cut horizontally, in circles, with the precision of a surgeon, so thin that I could see through the pieces.
 “It’s a root. It can grow five feet deep and 300kg,” he explained, in response to my shock. He sources it, he said, from the neighbouring state of Kerala, from people who “get it from the forests.” He hasn’t seen anybody extract the root, but has seen the tree it comes from. “It’s like a climber. It gives flowers. It grows near the sea. It’s called  Bhoochakara Gadda in south India and  Ram Kand Mool up north.”
 Can a root be this massive? Especially the root of a climber or vine? Before I could ask Google, my snack was ready. It was seasoned with salt, chili powder, and lime; my husband’s had sugar and lime. It was crunchy, juicy, and refreshing, but had no taste of its own.
 That night, I googled  Bhoochakara Gadda. There wasn’t much. Wikipedia identified the scientific name of the plant as  Maerua oblongifolia, but had no photos of it.  Maerua oblongifolia is a low, woody, undershrub found in India, Pakistan, parts of Africa, and Saudi Arabia, whose tubers are sold as snacks and used as a stimulant in the ancient medicine system of Siddha, I read. Research papers showed its leaves and flowers but not the root. So I decided to dig in.
  I contacted a wood scientist, a professor of food science, and a Siddha practitioner, each from my state. They hadn’t seen the tree. I pivoted my search to another state in the south, Andhra Pradesh. I called up its tribal department, but got no leads. Then my messages to the head of the biodiversity board were met with silence. I spoke to a botany researcher who had studied the tree, only to learn he had never seen it in person.
 Soon enough, I’d learn that my idle curiosity was, in fact, a mystery that has baffled and, at times, infuriated botanists for decades. This snack has been widely sold on the streets for decades—from the city of Haridwar in the north to Pune in the west and in several places in the south—and yet, somehow, no one seems to know what it is.
  I had a breakthrough a few months later when I came across a thesis paper by Dr. MS Rathore, who had propagated  Maerua oblongifolia in the lab in 2011. He had seen the tree many times in the desert state of Rajasthan. “But I haven’t heard or seen anybody eating the root,” the scientist said over a call, sounding puzzled.
 “Its roots are sparse and inedible,” added Dr. NS Shekhawat, his thesis adviser and a retired professor of botany. “Growing in dry regions, where will it have so much water to develop big roots and be fat and juicy? [The snack] can’t be  Maerua oblongifolia.”
 The duo sent me photos of the tree, which did not match the description on Wikipedia. And the roots in the photos were grown in a lab and too tiny to conclude anything.
 I spoke next to Dr. T Pullaiah, former president of The Indian Botanical Society. In his 2019  Encyclopaedia of World Medicinal Plants, he links this snack to   Ipomoea  d  igitata , a climber that grows a large tuberous root with a yellowish-brown coat. He must have seen the root, I thought. “No. It was second-hand knowledge. We are professors, busy between teaching and administrative work, so we rely on existing literature to come to conclusions,” he explained.
 So I made more calls, wrote more emails. And a new name came up that nullified all my research.
  The year was 1994. Ethnobotanist Dr. Koppula Hemadri was going around India “digging out roots” to confirm the origins of this snack. He ended his search with agave. These are succulents that look like aloe vera but can grow up to 10 feet wide and twice as tall. Some have a stout stem topped with spiny leaves, like pineapples. Some appear stemless.
 “I tasted the base of agave , that’s attached to roots,” remarked Dr. Hemadri, who’s now retired and lives in Andhra Pradesh. “It was starchy and a bit like that snack. But I did not pursue [the lead] after that.”
  In the same year, in the same state, botanist Dr. Ali Moulali got closer: “I told a vendor I would pay him Rs1,000-2,000 ($13-27) more if he revealed the identity of the plant. He hesitated and said, ‘It’s the base of  Kitta Nara.’ “ That’s what the fibre made from agave is locally called. The vendor also revealed that it wasn’t a root, but something that grew just above the ground.
 In the same period, flowering-plant taxonomist Dr. SR Yadav was encouraging his students in the western state of Maharashtra to scrutinise  Ram Kand, which is the snack’s local name there. Two of his students—Dr. Mansingraj S Nimbalkar and Dr. Vinod B Shimpale, who were studying molecular biology and taxonomy—went on to provide the most scientific insight into this mystery.
 In 2010, after a long anatomical study, they performed DNA barcoding on a slice of the snack and found it to match that of agave’s by 89 percent. There are several species of agave, but the lab test narrowed it down to  Agave Sisalana, a plant sometimes used to make a tequila-like drink . They did a field visit soon after and plucked out a  Sisalana only to find mesh-like, shallow roots. Next, they chopped off its leaves and there it was: the fat, white, watery trunk familiar to millions of Indians from food carts. They ate a slice from it, and it was tasteless and crunchy just like  Ram Kand. The findings were  published in Current Science the following year.
  So why is there still doubt about the identity of this snack? “Which species of agave is it— Sisalana or  Americana or any other?” Dr. Shimpale says. “We can’t conclude until the vendors show the plant to us. They keep this as a business secret to create curiosity around it.”
 Yes, there is a pattern to what the vendors say: It’s a root; it’s medicinal; they get it from a forest 200 kilometers away or in Africa. They say the Hindu god Ram, and his wife and brother, subsisted on  Ram Kand during their exile in the forests, and that  Bhoochakara Gadda is a sweet-something growing underground. Try to buy their stock in bulk and they’ll spare no more than a few slices. Probe them and they’ll cart away. “Forest officials in Maharashtra have tried to spy on them, but it was futile,” Dr. Nimbalkar recalls.
 Going on a hunch, I rang up a senior forest official in Karnataka, GS Yadav. “You need permission to remove or extract anything from any forest,” he says clearly. But agave doesn’t fully fit into the forest bracket. It grows widely in India, on roadsides, along railway tracks, as fencing. However, it may not be so healthy. “Agave has lots of alkaloids. It can be poisonous if eaten in large quantities. Maybe that’s why they sell thin slices,” Dr. Yadav, now retired, warns.
 I felt increasingly certain that agave was the answer until Dr. Chenna Kesava Reddy Sangati, an assistant professor of nutrition & technology in Bengaluru, dubbed it “impossible.” He has researched agave extensively in order to produce an alcoholic beverage from its  Albomarginata variety. “I have eaten  Bhoochakara Gadda. It has a smoother mouthfeel, is softer to bite, and is not very sweet,” he says. “Whereas this agave is highly sweet, astringent, fibrous, and hard to bite.”
   In April, the second wave of coronavirus in India had set in. Crowds on the street had thinned, push-carts were fewer, and the vendor I had first met was gone. “He has gone to his village,” a man who sells watermelons on the same street told me. But he passed on the vendor’s contact number.
 Fearing another lockdown, the vendor had returned to the northern state of Jharkhand. The pandemic was killing his business and he wanted to know from me when things will be normal. What could I say?
 We shifted the topic and he came clean easily: “It’s a stem … I sell it year-round … I have seen the plant only in photos, on the phone.” Oh! “Will you give me the phone number of the person you source  Bhoochakara Gadda from?” I asked. He went silent.
 “Ask anything but this, please. Nobody will tell you anything. This is how this business is,” he told me, getting irritated by the minute. Then he calmed down. “I’ve been eating this for years. It cools the body. Customers say it’s good for diabetes. It’s not illegal, I know.”
  After questioning my motives for calling him, he agreed to look at photos of the plants that experts were debating. “None,” he replied. I was gutted but called back to thank him. But then he said, “Photo number 3. That’s the plant.” It was  Agave Sisalana. He okayed Photo number 2 as well, which was  Agave Americana. There was no word on Photo No. 1,  Maerua oblongifolia.
 “They cut the leaves and rub red color on the trunk to preserve its white color,” he explained.
 So is this an agave snack, after all? I will wait for the final word, as Dr. MS Shekhawat, another botanist from Rajasthan, has assured me that after the pandemic is over, he will go into the field, uproot, slice, and taste a sample of each likely candidate, and resolve the mystery once and for all.
 Gastro Obscura covers the world’s most wondrous food and drink.  Sign up for our email, delivered twice a week.
title: 困扰植物学家数十年的神秘街头小吃
title_en: The Mysterious Street Snack That Has Baffled Botanists for Decades

比特币是当今使用最广泛的加密货币。这些涉及大量计算能力的数字货币不是由中央银行发行，而是由计算机“开采”，这些计算机执行验证交易的能源密集型工作并将其添加到数字分类账中，称为区块链.正如开采煤炭或黄金取决于谁先获得商品一样，数字采矿也是如此，在这种情况下，计算机相互竞争，成为第一个验证交易块并赢得奖励的人。随着每台新计算机争夺奖品，算法都会进行调整以使其更难获得。就像煤炭公司雇佣更多煤矿工人一样，加密矿工购买更多更快的计算机，形成一种军备竞赛，推动了对电力的巨大需求。一些发电商——发现向比特币采矿业务提供电力比向电网出售电力可以赚更多的钱——正在转移重点。拥有海狸县海狸谷核电站的能源港本月早些时候宣布，它将为俄亥俄州的比特币采矿数据中心提供核电。卢塞恩县萨斯奎哈纳蒸汽电站的所有者塔伦能源公司也在做同样的事情。该公司上个月表示，将开发一个数据中心来挖掘数字货币，该数据中心最多可使用 300 兆瓦，即核电站容量的 12%。
一些人将移动装置带入油田，连接他们的机器以使用天然气运行，天然气是石油产品的副产品，否则会被燃烧。其他人担心所有这些数字采矿的大量和不断增长的碳足迹 - 比特币最引人注目的助推器埃隆马斯克最近呼吁该行业解释其对气候变化的贡献 - 正试图寻找可再生能源来为他们的机器提供动力。今天，Scrubgrass 是一个 85 兆瓦的蓝色盒子，在 Scrubgrass Township 的山上有一个黑色的烟囱，看起来很像它在 1993 年首次开放时的样子——除了后面装满比特币矿工的拖车。该行动最初与一波此类工厂一起上线，这些工厂旨在解决宾夕法尼亚州废弃煤堆的遗留问题。这些工厂利用了 1990 年代的一项新技术——循环流化床，可以燃烧这种低品位材料并控制硫和一氧化二氮的排放。石灰石被注入到这个过程中，所产生的灰烬现在呈碱性，通常会散布在废煤来源的土地上以中和酸。去年，宾夕法尼亚州环境保护部估计，在过去的 30 年里，大约 3,700 英亩的土地被回收利用，其中大部分是在废煤发电厂燃烧的煤堆之后，该州还剩下大约 9,000 英亩的废煤堆。有些桩有数百英尺深。 “显然还有更多工作要做，”DEP 秘书帕特里克麦克唐纳去年在关于此类发电厂温室气体影响的听证会上对州立法委员会说。它们每单位能源排放的二氧化碳比普通燃煤电厂多，因为它们的燃料质量远低于纯煤。这一直是一个权衡——清理废煤堆可以清理周围的水，并修复难看和危险的土地。但是二氧化碳会进入空气中。
在 2019 年，即有可用联邦数据的最后一年，Scrubgrass 排放了 371,000 吨二氧化碳，相当于 80,000 辆汽车行驶一年的温室气体足迹。 2012 年，当工厂全速运转时，它的排放量接近 100 万吨。麦克唐纳先生去年说：“在剩下的垃圾堆中，大约有 40 个已经点燃并持续燃烧，严重影响了当地的空气质量，并释放了大量的二氧化碳和其他污染物。”其他估计表明燃烧堆的数量超过 90。这就是为什么 DEP 基本上免除废煤电厂购买碳信用额度作为其加入区域温室气体倡议（一个多州碳限额和交易计划）计划的一部分。最初是另一个人的病，他父亲的病，让斯宾塞先生涉足废煤行业。作为一名来自 Mon Valley 的采矿工程师，他在 1990 年代初期住在德克萨斯州，当时他的父亲生病了，Spence 先生开始长途旅行到匹兹堡照顾他。突然间，他小时候看惯的废煤堆看起来不一样了——它们看起来像是一个机会。 1994 年，斯宾塞先生在 West Deer 购买了 500 万吨的煤渣（即沥青垃圾），并与维南戈县的一家全新工厂签订了燃烧废煤的合同。花了十年的时间将所有这些材料运往北 60 英里到 Scrubgrass。被烧毁后，产生的灰烬用卡车运回西鹿并散布在土地上。它在那里又压实了十年。
本月早些时候，斯宾塞先生站在阿勒格尼县北部那片平坦的土地上，这里现在是一对足球场和一个室内运动场的所在地，他谈到了清理废煤后可能会发生的事情。然后他沿着这条路开了四分之一英里，在那里一堆废弃的煤废料表明还有多少工作要做。现在每天向 Scrubgrass 运送 50 辆卡车的另一个​​ West Deer 堆代表了一个煤矿的残余物，该煤矿曾经在第二次世界大战期间为建造美国摩天大楼和生产武器提供钢铁。该矿于 1904 年开工，八年后关闭，地上留下了成堆的废煤。每次下雨时，残留物都会将酸性重金属浸入土壤中，使溪流变成橙色。但在过去几个月中，在两轮融资中吸引投资者的并不是环境方面的善举，这些融资的收益超过 1 亿美元。 Stronghold Digital Mining 的首席执行官兼总裁 Greg Beard 表示，吸引力在于比特币业务，该公司是他与 Spence 先生创立的，旨在将 Scrubgrass 和其他几家废煤厂转变为加密货币中心。周二，Stronghold 向美国证券交易委员会提交了成为上市公司的文件。
2017 年，工厂工程师杰夫·坎贝尔从 Spence 先生家回家后就开始研究比特币。他观看了一段 40 分钟的 YouTube 视频，并说这个想法一针见血：“这是一种以权力为基础的货币。”在亚马逊上，他买了一个 50 美元的 U 盘，承诺开采比特币。他将自己的计算机切换到一个孤立的网络并插入。当时，比特币仍然是“边缘”，坎贝尔先生说，他担心会因为加密货币的声誉而感染计算机病毒甚至被 FBI 列入监视名单。为恐怖主义转移资金。 USB 记忆棒如宣传的那样发挥作用，几周后，坎贝尔先生投资 1,000 美元买了一台矿机，这是一台唯一用于运行计算的计算机。它运行了一周，产生了相当于 6.65 美元的比特币。坎贝尔先生紧张地将机器的数字钱包连接到 Scrubgrass 的 PNC 帐户以转移战利品，然后他走进办公室检查它是否真的出现在那里。 “我的天啊。我不敢相信这真的会奏效，”他说，“我们需要做的就是放入其中的 15,000 个，”他告诉斯宾塞先生。
根据坎贝尔先生的计算，以这个价格，用 15,000 台机器开采比特币将使工厂的营业收入增加约 50%。第一台机器的收益用于购买第二台机器，然后这两台机器用于购买第三台机器，依此类推。今天，大约有 3,000 名加密货币矿工装在发电厂后面的改装集装箱中，其中大部分归 Stronghold 所有，有些属于从发电厂购买电力的其他矿业公司。另外 5,000 台机器计划在下个月到达。根据提交给美国证券交易委员会的文件，Stronghold 计划在明年年底前运营 57,000 名矿工。 2020 年，当发电厂很少运行时，Stronghold 从其比特币业务中赚到的钱比通过将 Scrubgrass 的能源出售给电网赚的钱还多。今年前三个月，这种趋势发生了逆转。它从电力销售中获得了近 200 万美元，从其加密数据中心获得了超过 100 万美元。 Spence 先生与他的孩子们谈论区块链、涉及存储和验证大量数据的密码学、1960 年代人们谈论塑料的方式，就像电影“毕业生”一样。对于坎贝尔先生来说，感觉就像互联网的开始一样：他知道它将是革命性的和无处不在的，但愿景仍然模糊。 “面部识别？三维渲染？自动驾驶？人工智能？”他随地吐痰。 Spence 先生的商业伙伴 Beard 先生曾在 Apollo Global Management Inc. 管理能源投资，但他并不那么热情。
“我不确定你是否需要成为一个信徒，”他说。他将这些数字插入到 Excel 电子表格中，发现挖掘具有经济意义。这对他来说已经足够了。 Stronghold 正在购买另一家废煤厂，即位于碳县的 Panther Creek Energy Facility，并计划在那里复制其加密货币数据中心，并正在寻找第三家。虽然比特币是该操作的闪亮饰面，但它实际上是达到目的的一种手段——让 Scrubgrass 有理由运行超过电网需求的运行，以便它可以继续燃烧废煤。在最初的 20 年里，该工厂几乎不间断地运转。它与当地公用事业公司签订了电力购买协议，这意味着其输出有保证的需求和保证的价格。当这一切在 2013 年结束时，Scrubgrass 努力在竞争激烈的电力市场中挣扎，电力价格下降的部分原因是马塞勒斯页岩使天然气成为比煤炭更便宜的电力燃料，更不用说废煤了。植物——尤其是像 Scrubgrass 这样的小型植物，它们发现自己处于边缘状态——通常只在电网需求提高价格足以使其值得的高峰时间运行。
拥有持续的需求，例如附加的数据中心，意味着 Scrubgrass 不需要在电力价格下降时关闭。这也意味着，当电网需要时，Scrubgrass 可以像电池一样工作——立即将其电源切换到电网。 “我认为 10 年后，人们会说，'比特币是发电厂用来调节电网的东西，”坎贝尔先生说。他已经在想办法将矿工产生的热量送回发电厂。 （去年冬天，坎贝尔先生用比特币机器为他的家取暖）。与此同时，植物虽然没有春鸡，但感觉它仍在试图弄清楚它是什么——一个人们修补和实验的地方。 Shaffer 先生的大部分职业生涯都在该工厂度过，他自豪地宣称他的同事不是“典型的电厂人员”。一位前餐厅经理负责运营数据中心。除了多年来从事天然气服务和健康杂志的企业家之外，斯宾塞先生并不是一个典型的人，他在家前门内挂了带框的 Scrubgrass 照片。 “他们应该建造更多这样的工厂，而不是更少，”他最近说。 “这并不完美。我向你承认这一点。但这该死的好。”更正：在这个故事的早期版本中，《公报》错误地估计了工厂比特币开采的潜在收入。
note_en:
Bitcoin is the most widespread of the cryptocurrencies in use today. These digital currencies, which involve a huge amount of computing power, aren’t issued by a central bank but are instead “mined” by computers that perform the energy-intensive work of validating transactions and adding them to a digital ledger, called the blockchain.
 Just as mining coal or gold is a matter of who gets to the commodity first, so too is digital mining, where computers race against each other to be the first to validate a block of transactions and win their reward.
 With each new computer vying for the prize, the algorithm adjusts to make getting it more difficult.
 Rather like a coal company hiring more coal miners, crypto miners buy more and faster computers, creating a kind of arms race that’s driving a huge demand for power.
  Already, some power generators — finding they can make more money supplying electricity to Bitcoin-mining operations than selling it to the grid — are shifting focus.
 Energy Harbor, which owns the Beaver Valley Nuclear Plant in Beaver County, announced earlier this month that it will supply nuclear power to a Bitcoin-mining data center in Ohio.
 Talen Energy, owner of the Susquehanna Steam Electric Station in Luzerne County, is doing the same. The company said last month that it will develop a data center to mine digital currency that could use up to 300 megawatts, or 12% of the nuclear plant’s capacity.
  Some are taking mobile units into the oil fields, hooking up their machines to run on natural gas, a byproduct of oil product that would otherwise be flared. Others, worried about the substantial and growing carbon footprint of all this digital mining — Bitcoin’s highest profile booster Elon Musk recently called the industry to account for its contribution to climate change — are trying to find renewable sources of energy to power their machines.
            Today, Scrubgrass, an 85-megawatt blue box with a black smokestack in the hills of Scrubgrass Township, looks much like it did when it first opened in 1993 — except for the trailers filled with Bitcoin miners in the back.
 The operation originally came online along with a wave of such plants that were supposed to tackle Pennsylvania’s legacy of abandoned coal piles.
 The plants took advantage of a new technology in the 1990s — a circulating fluidized bed that made it possible to burn such low-grade material and control emissions of sulfur and nitrous oxides. Limestone is injected into the process, and the resulting ash, now alkaline, is often spread on the land where the waste coal came from to neutralize the acid.
  Last year, the Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection estimated there are about 9,000 acres filled with waste coal piles in the state remaining, after some 3,700 acres have been reclaimed over the past three decades, mostly by the piles being burned in waste coal power plants. Some piles are hundreds of feet deep.
 “There is clearly more work to be done,” DEP Secretary Patrick McDonnell told a state legislative committee last year during a hearing on the greenhouse gas impacts of such power plants. They emit more carbon dioxide than regular coal plants per unit of energy because the quality of their fuel is much lower than pure coal.
 That has always been the trade-off — cleaning up the waste coal piles cleans up the water around them and remediates unsightly and dangerous land. But the CO2 goes in the air.
 In 2019, the last year with available federal data, Scrubgrass emitted the equivalent of 371,000 tons of CO2 — the greenhouse gas footprint of 80,000 cars driving for a year. In 2012, when the plant was running at full force, it emitted close to a million tons.
  “Of the piles that remain,” Mr. McDonnell said last year, “approximately 40 have ignited and continually burn, significantly impacting local air quality and releasing significant amounts of carbon dioxide and other pollutants.” Other estimates put the number of burning piles at more than 90.
 That’s why the DEP essentially excused waste coal plants from buying carbon credits as part of its plan to join the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative, a multistate carbon cap-and-trade program.
            It was another man’s illness, his father’s, that brought Mr. Spence into the waste coal business in the first place. A mining engineer from the Mon Valley, he was living in Texas in the early 1990s when his father got sick and Mr. Spence began to take extended trips to Pittsburgh to care for him.
 Suddenly, the waste coal piles that he was used to seeing as a kid looked different — they looked like an opportunity.
 In 1994, Mr. Spence bought a 5 million-ton gob — that is, garbage of bituminous — pile in West Deer and secured a contract with a brand new plant in Venango County to burn the waste coal.
 It took a decade to truck all of that material 60 miles north to Scrubgrass. After it was burned, the resulting ash was trucked back to West Deer and spread on the land. There it sat compacting for another decade.
  Earlier this month, Mr. Spence stood on that flattened ground in northern Allegheny County, now home to a pair of soccer fields and an indoor sports complex, and talked about what’s possible when waste coal is cleaned up.
 Then he drove a quarter mile down the road, where a mountain range of abandoned coal waste showed how much is left to be done.
 The other West Deer pile that now sends 50 trucks to Scrubgrass every day represents the remnants of a coal mine that once supplied steel to build U.S. skyscrapers and produce weapons during World War II. The mine opened in 1904 and shuttered eight decades later, leaving heaps of waste coal on the ground.
 Every time it rains, the remnants leach an acidic brew of heavy metals into the earth, turning streams orange.
 But it’s not the environmental good-doing that hooked investors during two funding rounds that yielded more than $100 million over the past several months.
 The appeal was the Bitcoin operation, said Greg Beard, CEO and president of Stronghold Digital Mining, a company that he founded with Mr. Spence to turn Scrubgrass and several other waste coal plants into a crypto hub.
 On Tuesday, Stronghold filed documents with the Securities & Exchange Commission to become a public company.
            Jeff Campbell, the plant engineer, started researching Bitcoin as soon as he got home from Mr. Spence’s house in 2017. He watched a 40-minute YouTube video and said the idea clicked into place: “This is currency that’s underpinned by power.”
 On Amazon, he bought a $50 USB stick that promised to mine Bitcoin. He switched his computer to an isolated network and plugged it in.
 At that time, Bitcoin was still “fringe,” Mr. Campbell said, and he worried about getting a computer virus or even ending up on an FBI watchlist because of cryptocurrency’s reputation in moving funds for terrorism.
  The USB stick worked as advertised, and after a few weeks, Mr. Campbell invested $1,000 and bought a mining machine, a computer whose sole purpose is to run computations. It ran for a week and generated the equivalent of $6.65 in Bitcoin.
 Nervously, Mr. Campbell linked the machine’s digital wallet to Scrubgrass’ PNC account to transfer the spoils, then he went into the office to check if it really showed up there.
 “Oh, my God. I can’t believe this is really going to work,” he said
 “All we need to do is put 15,000 of these in,” he told Mr. Spence.
 According to Mr. Campbell’s calculations, at that price, mining for Bitcoin with 15,000 machines would add about 50% to the plant’s operating revenue.
 The earnings from the first machine funded the purchase of the second, then those two funded the third and so on.
 Today, there are about 3,000 cryptocurrency miners packed into retrofitted shipping containers behind the power plant, most of them owned by Stronghold and some that belong to other mining companies that buy power from the plant. Another 5,000 machines are scheduled to arrive next month. According to documents filed with the SEC, Stronghold is planning to operate 57,000 miners by the end of next year.
  In 2020, when the power plant seldom ran, Stronghold made more money from its Bitcoin operations than by selling Scrubgrass’s energy to the grid. During the first three months of this year, the trend reversed. It received almost $2 million from power sales and more than $1 million from its crypto datacenter.
 Mr. Spence talks to his kids about blockchain, the cryptography involved in storing and verifying huge swaths of data, the way people in the 1960s talked about plastics, a la the movie “The Graduate.”
  For Mr. Campbell it feels like the beginning of the internet did: He knows it’s going to be revolutionary and ubiquitous, but the vision is still fuzzy. “Facial recognition? Three-dimensional rendering? Autonomous driving? Artificial intelligence?” he spitballs.
 Mr. Spence’s business partner, Mr. Beard, who used to manage energy investing at Apollo Global Management Inc., isn’t as exuberant.
 “I’m not sure that you need to be a believer,” he said.
 He plugged the numbers into an Excel spreadsheet and saw that it makes economic sense to mine. That was enough for him.
 Stronghold is buying another waste coal plant, Panther Creek Energy Facility in Carbon County, with plans to replicate its cryptomining data center there, and is eyeing a third.
            While Bitcoin is the shiny veneer of the operation, it’s actually a means to an end — giving Scrubgrass a reason to run more than the electric grid needs so it can continue to burn waste coal.
 For the first 20 years, the plant ran nearly constantly. It had a power purchase agreement with the local utility, which meant there was a guaranteed demand and a guaranteed price for its output.
 When that ended, in 2013, Scrubgrass struggled to navigate the competitive power market, where the price of power was falling in part because the Marcellus Shale was making natural gas a cheaper fuel for electricity than coal, let alone waste coal.
 Plants — especially smaller ones like Scrubgrass that find themselves on the margin — often run only at peak times when the demand on the grid raises prices enough to make it worth their while.
 Having a constant demand, like the attached data center, means Scrubgrass doesn’t need to shut down when prices for power fall. It also means that when the grid needs it, Scrubgrass can act like a battery — instantly switching its power to the grid.
  “I think 10 years from now, people are going to say, ‘Bitcoin is the thing that power plants do to regulate the grid,” Mr. Campbell said.
 He’s already thinking of ways to route the heat produced by the miners back into the power plant. (Last winter, Mr. Campbell heated his home with Bitcoin machines).
 Meanwhile, the plant, although no spring chicken, feels like it’s still trying to figure out what it is — a place where people tinker and experiment. Mr. Shaffer, who spent most of his career at the plant, proudly proclaims that his colleagues aren’t “typical power plant people.” A former restaurant manager runs the data center.
 Mr. Spence, not a typical anything except an entrepreneur whose ventures over the years ranged from natural gas services to a health magazine, hung the framed photo of Scrubgrass just inside the front door of his home.
 “They should be building more plants like this, not less,” he said recently. “It’s not perfect. I acknowledge that to you. But it’s damn good.”
  Correction: In an earlier version of this story, the Post-Gazette incorrectly estimated potential revenue from bitcoin mining at the plant.
title: 为什么废煤发电厂正在“为比特币燃烧”
title_en: Why a Waste-Coal Power Plant is 'Burning for Bitcoin'

路透北京8月6日 - 北京检方周五对腾讯(0700.HK)子公司提起民事公益诉讼，称该公司广受欢迎的社交消息应用微信的“青春模式”不符合保护未成年人的法律.根据中国最高检察官运营的网站 JCRB.com 上发布的文件，该诉讼是由北京市海淀区人民检察院对深圳市腾讯计算机系统有限公司提起的。该文件没有说明微信的“青年模式”如何违反中国法律。腾讯没有立即回应置评请求。微信的“青春模式”开启后，会限制年轻用户使用一些游戏和功能，例如支付或寻找附近的朋友。中国当局呼吁更好地保护未成年人免受网络危险，本周官方媒体也回应了这一观点，批评视频游戏行业以及有助于推广名人文化的在线平台。腾讯周二宣布了对未成年人访问其旗舰视频游戏“王者荣耀”的新限制，此前其股价受到一篇将网络游戏描述为“精神鸦片”的官方媒体文章的重创。阅读更多 路透社 4 月援引知情人士的话报道称，中国正准备对腾讯处以巨额罚款，作为对中国互联网巨头进行全面反垄断打击的一部分，但可能低于创纪录的 27.5 亿美元罚款阿里巴巴（9988.HK）。
（这个故事更正说北京检察官提起诉讼，而不是在第 1-2 段中提起） 北京的 Yilei Sun 和 Brenda Goh 以及香港的 Twinnie Siu 由 Susan Fenton 和 Andrew Heavens 编辑
note_en:
BEIJING, Aug 6 (Reuters) - Beijing prosecutors initiated a civil public-interest lawsuit against a Tencent  (0700.HK) subsidiary on Friday, saying the "youth mode" on the company's popular social messaging app WeChat does not comply with laws protecting minors.
 The lawsuit was initiated by Beijing’s Haidian District People’s Procuratorate against Shenzhen Tencent Computer Systems Co Ltd, according to a filing posted on JCRB.com, a website run by China’s top prosecutor.
 The filing did not say how WeChat's "youth mode" broke Chinese law. Tencent did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
 WeChat's "youth mode", when turned on, limits young users' access to some games and functions, such as payments or finding nearby friends.
 Chinese authorities have called for minors to be better protected from online dangers, a sentiment echoed by state media this week which criticised the video gaming industry as well as online platforms that help promote celebrity culture.
 Tencent on Tuesday announced new curbs on minors' access to its flagship video game, "Honor of Kings", after its shares were battered by a state media article that described online games as "spiritual opium".   read more
 Reuters reported in April, citing people familiar with the matter, that China is preparing a substantial fine for Tencent as part of its sweeping antitrust clampdown on the country's internet giants, but it is likely to be less than the record $2.75 billion penalty imposed on Alibaba  (9988.HK).
 (This story corrects to say Beijing prosecutors initiated the lawsuit, not filed in paragraph 1-2)
 Reporting by Yilei Sun and Brenda Goh in Beijing, and Twinnie Siu in Hong KongEditing by Susan Fenton and Andrew Heavens
title: 中国起诉腾讯，称微信“青春模式”不符合国家保护未成年人法律
title_en: China sues Tencent, claiming WeChat's “youth mode” does not comply with the country's laws protecting minors

我讨厌处理图像。这是制作网站和撰写博客文章中最乏味的部分。事实上，有时我非常害怕它，以至于我不发布帖子，因为我必须为他们制作资产。如果你集中精力制作它们，它就是一个上传和重新上传的循环，直到你获得正确的分辨率、大小和颜色。 Figma 是一种用于制作模型的设计工具。它内置的一项功能是能够将您的设计导出为图像。因此，对于我的大部分横幅、图形以及几乎所有用于网络的内容，我都将它们制作为 Figma。每次我进行更改时，我都必须将图片导出并上传。如果尺寸不正确，或颜色错误，我必须将其拖过来并上传到其他地方。这也意味着你会得到一堆相同的资产，称为“image”、“image_copy_1”、“image_copy_2”。
省去这整个麻烦，您现在可以直接在 Figma 中获得指向您设计的图像链接。事实上，这篇博文中的所有图片都保存在一个 Figma 文件中，并已上传到 Figmage。如果圣诞节快到了，我希望每张图片都有红色背景，我可以在不触及我的网站的情况下进行和发布更改。变化是瞬间可见的。我使用 AWS Lambda 和 S3 构建了 Figmage 的图像托管。作为一名技术人员，我必须权衡利弊： 托管在 Figmage.com 上 - 图像托管在 figmage 域而不是您的自定义域中。 Webflow 做了类似的事情。这可能适用于图像的 SEO，也可能不适用于图像。冷启动 - 我通常发现图像需要 200 毫秒的加载时间。这是 AWS Lambda 的一般响应时间。但是，在冷启动情况下（例如没有人使用该服务），发送图像可能需要长达 2 秒的时间。因此，对于加载至关重要的主要资产（例如背景），我可能不会使用 Figmage。无论如何，您始终可以将最终资产上传到您选择的 Figma 提供商。当您仍在尝试正确的资产时，您可以将 figmage 用作游乐场。
这也是我第一个盈利的项目，你可以以 20 美元的价格获得 1000 张图像的终身有限交易。
note_en:
I hate working with images. It’s the most tedious part of making websites and writing blog posts.
 In fact, sometimes I dread it so much, that I don’t publish posts because I have to make the assets for them.
  And if you muster up the energy to make them, it’s a cycle of uploads and re-uploads till you get the right resolution, size and colors.
  Figma is a design tool that’s used to make mockups. One feature built-into it is the ability to export your designs as an image.
 So for most of my banners, and graphics and almost anything for the web, I make them Figma.
  And everytime I make changes, I have to export the the picture out, and upload it.
 If a size is incorrect, or a color is wrong, I have to drag it over and upload it somewhere else. That also means you get a bunch of the same assets called “image”, “image_copy_1”, “image_copy_2”.
  Taking this entire hassle out, you can now just get an image link directly to your design in Figma.
     In fact, all of the images in this blog post live in a Figma file and have been uploaded to Figmage.
 If Christmas rolls around, and I wanted every image to have a red background, I can make and publish the changes without even touching my website. The changes are seen in an instant.
   I built Figmage’s image hosting using AWS Lambda and S3. And as a technology person, I have to call out the trade-offs:
 Hosted on Figmage.com - The images are hosted with the figmage domain rather than your custom domain. Webflow does something similar. This may or may not work well with SEO for images.
 Cold-Start - I’ve generally found that images take 200ms load. Which is the general response time for AWS Lambda. However, in a cold-start (e.g. nobody is using the service), it may take up-to 2 seconds to send an image. So for major assets that are critical to load (e.g. a background), I might not use Figmage.
 In any case, you can always upload your final assets to a provider of your choice from Figma. You can use figmage as a playground for when you’re still experimenting with the right assets.
 It’s also my first project that makes revenue, you can get a  limited lifetime deal for a 1000 images at $20.
title: Figmage：我不搞乱图片
title_en: Figmage: I don't mess with images

跳转到导航 跳转到搜索 特雷弗·保罗·摩尔（Trevor Paul Moore，1980 年 4 月 4 日 - 2021 年 8 月 6 日）是美国喜剧演员、演员、作家、导演、制片人和音乐家。他被称为纽约市喜剧团 The Whitest Kids U' Know 的创始成员之一，与 Sam Brown 和 Zach Cregger 一起，他们在 IFC 上有自己的小品喜剧系列，连续五个赛季。摩尔出生于新泽西州的蒙特克莱尔。 [2] 他的父母是前基督教民谣摇滚歌手 Mickey 和 Becki Moore，他们在 1980 年代取得了成功，他们的单曲“Love Song for Number Two”在美国排名第二。 [3] [4] 因为他和家人经常旅行，所以他经常换学校，去了大约五所不同的学校。 [5] [6] 15岁那年，他将自己的早期作品汇编成一本名为《Scraps》的书，成为一名出版漫画家。 [7] [8] 16 岁时，摩尔为现已解散的《夏洛茨维尔观察家报》创作了漫画《卡迪》。特雷弗·摩尔 (Trevor Moore) 在夏洛茨维尔 (Charlottesville) 的圣约学校 (Covenant School) 上高中，到 18 岁时，他毕业的同时也在开发个人项目。 [7] 摩尔最初是弗吉尼亚联邦大学的广播专业，虽然他最初想学习新闻学和政治学，但他最终选择了电影。 [9] 摩尔，就像他未来的白人孩子一样，萨姆·布朗和扎克·克雷格，在曼哈顿著名的 SVA 视觉艺术学院学习，在那里他主修电影，并以优等成绩毕业。 [10] 从 1997 年到 1998 年，他的节目名为 The Trevor Moore Show，在弗吉尼亚州夏洛茨维尔的公共电视上播出。它在当地大学社区中获得了追随者，因此在他 18 岁时，Pax-TV（现在的 Ion 电视）向摩尔提供了一笔交易。 [3] 该节目持续了 16 集，其中包括“我想知道今天谁死了？”这样的草图。 （来自当地老年人之家的恶搞新闻广播），它还以“Walking-Talking Box”为特色。但由于被认为是令人反感的材料，主要是因为节目的节目编排错误，除了晚间节目表外，它的播出时间也太早，无法达到收视率，所以它被取消了。 [7] 他认为该节目只会在晚上播出，但在第一季进行到一半时，他发现它会在周六早上 9 点重播。 [7] 后来，摩尔继续在有线电视初创公司 ImaginAsianTV 工作，担任 Jimbo Matison 的莫蒂叔叔的 Dub Shack 的制片人和编剧，这是一部喜剧节目，涉及喜剧演员表演小品，并重新配音和模仿亚洲老电影。 [11]
到 2002 年，在他大学的最后一年，摩尔获得了周六夜现场的个人实习机会。他只打算在那里呆一个学期，但他们最终要求他呆一整年。这让他进入了梦寐以求的 NBC Page Program，该计划收到大约 50,000 份申请，一年只需要 50 人。 [3] 他将《周六夜现场》的创作者和执行制片人 Lorne Michaels 归功于他的喜剧教育。 [7] [10] 在那之后，他在那里巡演了大约一年。 2004 年，摩尔的喜剧团 Whitest Kids U' Know 开始定期在下东区的 Pianos 酒吧订婚。 [7] 2008 年，摩尔在 HBO 节目 The Life and Times of Tim 的一集中担任客座配音。 2009 年，摩尔出现在 WWE 的草图中，在 12 轮比赛中是约翰塞纳的“替身”。 WKUK 在互联网和现场表演上的成功使他们受邀参加了在阿斯彭举行的 2006 年 HBO 美国喜剧艺术节。剧团没有让人失望，获得了最佳素描组奖，吸引了众多好莱坞高管的目光。 [7] 在 The Whitest Kids U'Know 取得成功后，福克斯探照灯找到了摩尔和扎克·克雷格的剧本，并为他们提供了一个电影项目。经考虑接受后，将原剧本改写为喜剧风格，并在完成《最白的孩子》第二季的拍摄后，执导并主演了《三月小姐》。这是摩尔的第一部故事片。它于 2009 年 3 月 13 日上映。 [12] [13] 在 The Whitest Kids U' Know 在 HBO 美国喜剧节上获胜之后； Sundance、MTV 和 Comedy Central 都在和他们讨论做试播，但 Fuse 已经开始了。它现在在世界范围内进行联合。 [10]
在大学期间，摩尔和山姆·布朗萌生了拍摄一部关于美国内战的电影的想法。最后，在为你所知道的最白的孩子们拍摄第五季时，他和剧团编写并拍摄了他的第二部故事片“毒品内战”，他们都扮演了多个角色。这部电影由特雷弗·摩尔和扎克·克雷格执导。它在影院有限上映，并最终在 WKUK 第五季播出。这是 WKUK 为记录内战期间大麻合法化之旅而制作的历史剧。 [5] [14] 近年来，摩尔定期出现在与杰·雷诺 (Jay Leno) 的今夜秀中，反复出现的片段展示了预先录制好的街头风格喜剧片段，其中包括恶作剧和与毫无戒心的公众相遇。 [16] [17] 摩尔还在各种场合与滑稽或死亡和喜剧中心合作。 [18] 自从WKUK喜剧团成立以来，摩尔和其他成员不断地在不同项目中以个人或团体的形式参与舞台表演。他每年都与 WKUK 剧团一起巡回演出，现场表演新旧草图。 [3] 摩尔不时与纽约和好莱坞的其他喜剧演员一起参加名为 Whatev'r Show 的现场表演。 [19] 从2012年11月6日开始，每个月的第一个星期二在总统选举当晚以特别节目开场，到2013年2月，特雷弗·摩尔与喜剧演员乔希一起在舞台上进行了脱口秀和喜剧表演。在洛杉矶风靡一时。该节目被称为特雷弗·摩尔做脱口秀节目和乔什·法登一起做其他一些事情的节目……还有更多。 [20] 摩尔于 2013 年 3 月从 Comedy Central 发行了他的首张专辑，名为 Drunk Texts to Myself。他为这张同样由喜剧中心制作的专辑导演并主演了补充音乐视频。专辑有 12 首曲目，例如“Drunk Texts To Myself (feat. Reggie Watts)”、“What About Mouthwash?”，以及可以说是专辑中最受欢迎的歌曲“Founding Fathers Rap”。 [21]
'Drunk Texts to Myself' 是摩尔用各种音乐形式表现社会不同情况下矛盾的方式，从说唱和金属到乡村和流行。 [21] 他和一些朋友在美国巡回演出时演奏了这张专辑。 [3] [21] 摩尔于 2015 年 3 月 10 日在 Comedy Central 发行了他的第二张专辑《High in Church》。这张专辑包含现场歌曲和新歌。独特的歌曲包括阴谋论寓言的“Kitty History”、讽刺的乡村歌曲“The Gays Got Married”和探讨 YouTube 恶意评论本质的“The Ballad of Billy John”。摩尔在 4 点 20 分从喜剧中心发行了他的第三张专辑，名为《我们时代的故事》。与之前的专辑不同，之前发行的歌曲没有现场演绎。主题多种多样，但包括 YouTube 名人、真人秀和在线巨魔的愚蠢。摩尔与 The Whitest Kids U' Know 的另一位创始成员 Sam Brown 在 Comedy Central 制作了一部迷你剧，该剧由三集组成，并于 2019 年 8 月开始播出。 [22] 特雷弗·摩尔也是迪斯尼频道节目的创作者名为 Just Roll With It，于 2019 年 6 月首映。 [23] [循环参考] 特雷弗·摩尔和剧团的其他成员正在开发一部 WKUK 故事片。 [24] 在 COVID-19 大流行期间，摩尔在 YouTube 上主持了特雷弗·摩尔隔离秀 [25]，其中经常包括与他和他的狗的素描，然后是与 WKUK 老演员们讨论随机话题的录制直播会话. [26]

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  Trevor Paul Moore (April 4, 1980 – August 6, 2021) was an American comedian, actor, writer, director, producer, and musician. He was known as one of the founding members, alongside  Sam Brown and  Zach Cregger, of the  New York City-based comedy troupe   The Whitest Kids U' Know, who had their own  sketch comedy series on  IFC which ran for five seasons.
   Moore was born in  Montclair, New Jersey.  [2] His parents are former Christian folk-rock singers, Mickey and Becki Moore, who were successful in the 1980s, their single 'Love Song for Number Two' having ranked as the number two Christian song in the USA.  [3]  [4] Because he traveled a lot on tour with his family, he changed schools constantly, going to about five different schools.  [5]  [6] By the age of 15, he became a published cartoonist after compiling his early work in a book called  Scraps.  [7]  [8] At 16, Moore created the comic strip Cuddy for the now-defunct newspaper  The Charlottesville Observer.
 Trevor Moore attended high school at the  Covenant School in Charlottesville, and by the time he was 18 he graduated while also developing personal projects.  [7]
 Moore started out as a broadcasting major at  Virginia Commonwealth University, and while he originally wanted to study journalism and political science, he chose film in the end.  [9] Moore, like his future   Whitest Kids U' Know comrades  Sam Brown and  Zach Cregger, studied in Manhattan's prestigious SVA  School of Visual Arts where he majored in film with a BFA and graduated  cum laude.  [10]
   From 1997 to 1998 his show, called  The Trevor Moore Show, ran on  public-access television in  Charlottesville, Virginia. It garnered a following among the local college community so by the time he was 18, Moore was offered a deal by Pax-TV (now  Ion Television).  [3] The show lasted sixteen episodes with sketches like "I Wonder Who Died Today?" (a parody newscast from the local senior citizens' home), it also featured the "Walking-Talking Box." but it was cancelled due to what was deemed offensive material and mostly to a mistake on the programming of the show that besides its night schedule was also being broadcast too early for its rating.  [7]
 It was his belief that the show would only air at night, but halfway through the first season he found out that it was being re-run at 9 AM Saturday mornings.  [7]
 Later, Moore went on to work at the cable TV start up ImaginAsianTV as a producer and writer for Jimbo Matison's  Uncle Morty's Dub Shack, a comedy show that involved comedians performing sketches, and re-voicing and parodying old Asian movies.  [11]
  By 2002, on his last year of college, Moore got the personal internship to   Saturday Night Live. He was going to be there only for one semester, but they ended up asking him to stay the entire year. This got him into the coveted NBC Page Program, which gets about 50,000 applications and only takes 50 people a year.  [3] He credits  Saturday Night Live creator and executive producer  Lorne Michaels as part of his comic education.  [7]  [10] He did tours there for about a year after that.
 In 2004, Moore's comedy troupe,   Whitest Kids U' Know, started a regular engagement at the  Lower East Side bar, Pianos.  [7]
 In 2008, Moore was a guest voice on an episode of the HBO show   The Life and Times of Tim.
 In 2009, Moore was featured in a sketch for  WWE, on the set of   12 Rounds being  John Cena's "hand double".
 The WKUK success on the internet and live shows led to an invitation to the 2006  HBO  U.S. Comedy Arts Festival in Aspen. The troupe did not disappoint, winning the award for Best Sketch Group and attracting the attention of many Hollywood executives.  [7]
 After the success of  The Whitest Kids U'Know, Fox Searchlight approached Moore and Zach Cregger with a script and offered them a movie project. After consideration they accepted, rewrote the original script and adapted it to their comedy style, and after completing filming of the second season of  The Whitest Kids U'Know, they directed and starred in   Miss March. This was Moore's first feature film. It was released on March 13, 2009.  [12]  [13]
  After  The Whitest Kids U' Know won at the HBO U.S. Comedy Festival; Sundance, MTV, and Comedy Central were all talking to them about doing a pilot, but  Fuse was already getting it started. It is now in syndication around the world.  [10]
 During their college years, Moore and Sam Brown had the idea for a movie about the  American Civil War. Finally, while shooting the fifth season for the Whitest Kids U' Know, he and the troupe wrote and filmed his second feature film titled  "The Civil War on Drugs" where they all played multiple roles. The movie was directed by Trevor Moore and Zach Cregger. It was limitedly released in theaters and ultimately run along the WKUK fifth season. It is a historical drama that the WKUK made to document the journey to legalize marijuana during the Civil War.  [5]  [14]
  In recent years, Moore was periodically featured on   The Tonight Show with Jay Leno in recurring segments showcasing pre-taped man-on-the-street style comedy bits which feature pranks on and encounters with an unsuspecting public.  [16]  [17]
 Moore has also collaborated in various occasions with  Funny or Die and The Comedy Central.  [18]
 Since the foundation of the WKUK comedy troupe, Moore and the other members constantly participate in on stage presentations either individually or as a group in different projects. He toured every year with the WKUK troupe performing in live shows with old and new sketches.  [3]
 From time to time Moore performed in live shows called the  Whatev'r Show along other comedians in NYC and Hollywood.  [19]
 On the first Tuesday of every month from November 6, 2012, when it opened with a special show on the night of the presidential election, through February 2013, Trevor Moore did a talk show and comedy show on stage with fellow comedian  Josh Fadem in LA. The show was called  The Show Where Trevor Moore Does a Talk Show Thing and Josh Fadem Does Some Other Stuff Too All In One... Plus More.  [20]
 Moore released his debut album from Comedy Central in March 2013 called  Drunk Texts to Myself. He directed and starred in complementary musical videos for this album also produced by Comedy Central. The album has 12 tracks, such as "Drunk Texts To Myself (feat. Reggie Watts)", "What About Mouthwash?", and arguably the most popular song on the album, "Founding Fathers Rap".  [21]
 'Drunk Texts to Myself' is Moore's way to represent contradiction in different instances of society using a variety of musical forms, going from rap and metal to country and pop.  [21] He's performed the album along with some friends on a tour around the USA.  [3]  [21]
 Moore released his second album from Comedy Central on March 10, 2015 called  High in Church. This album contained live and new songs. Unique songs include "Kitty History" a parable of  conspiracy theories, "The Gays Got Married", a sardonic country song, and "The Ballad of Billy John" which explores the nature of  malicious YouTube comments.
 Moore released his third album from Comedy Central on   4:20 called  The Story of Our Times. Unlike the previous album there are no live renditions of previously released songs. Subject matter is varied but includes the inanity of  youtube celebrities,  reality television and  online trolls.
 Moore made a miniseries on Comedy Central with another founding member of The Whitest Kids U' Know, Sam Brown, that consisted of three episodes and started airing in August 2019.  [22]
 Trevor Moore is also the creator of a show on the Disney Channel called  Just Roll With It that premiered in June 2019.  [23] [   circular reference]
 Trevor Moore and the other members of the troupe were developing a WKUK feature film.  [24]
 During the  COVID-19 pandemic, Moore hosted  The Trevor Moore Quarantine Show,  [25] on YouTube, which often involved a sketch with he and his dog, followed by a recorded live stream session with old WKUK cast members discussing random topics.  [26]

有没有人觉得“过你最好的单身生活”和“让自己快乐”的建议适得其反，尤其是对于 30 多岁的单身女性？我：37个大城市的女职业。一些花哨的学位。一份稳定、高收入的工作。拥有自己的公寓。有趣/古怪的爱好（登山、骑自行车、摄影）。经常去奇怪的地方旅行，通常是一个人（大学毕业后去了Peace Corps，所以在国外独自一人很舒服）。拥有不同人生阶段的朋友。做了几年的治疗，所以我想我至少有一些自我意识。 （因为人们会问，我在匹配/约会时没有问题，所以我不认为我的外表是我仍然单身的唯一原因。而且我使用最近的照片。）我几乎拥有我一直想要的生活，合作伙伴除外。我一直在努力构建它。但我觉得我的生活越成功和有趣，男人对我作为长期合作伙伴的兴趣就越少。我遇到的男人越少，就越能以有意义的方式为我的生活增添光彩。但仅仅因为我一个人比较快乐，不需要“需要”伴侣/去做我想做的一切，而不用等待有人陪我去（旅行，买房等），这并不意味着我不想爱上下一个人。我很想有个人一起分担起起落落；一起探索世界；成为队友，共创人生；哦，定期满足性行为也非常好。然而，似乎我越是“为自己努力”让我现在的生活变得美好，它就越使我与潜在的合作伙伴疏远。我曾让男人告诉我，我很容易走开，因为我“不需要”他们。我曾有过男人说我的生活太完整/充实而无法拥有伴侣——就像他们在其中没有立足之地。但对我来说，需要某人和等待某人只是你过着不快乐/健康的单身生活的迹象。我觉得我所做的所有事情都是为了让自己快乐、健康和成功（在工作中获得晋升；去墨西哥爬一些火山；拜访我住在不同城市的大学朋友；报名参加 100 -英里自行车骑行）正在积极阻碍我的约会前景，因为乔、山姆或巴勃罗根本不重视这些东西。相反，我看到那些说他们想要像我这样的女人的男人实际上选择了那些花更多时间在外表上（头发、标记、时尚）和更多时间观看 Bravo、做瑜伽、拥抱他们的金毛的人。这些女人正在等待一个男人独自旅行或做任何事情，并且通常对此很诚实。 （在人们告诉我之前，如果我只是过我的生活，我会在我最不期望的时候遇到某个人：好吧，我做到了，但我没有。我不经常通过旅行或爱好遇到单身、有魅力的男人。大多数男人我遇到是搭档。单身的人最终认为我是一个肾上腺素高的伙伴，并会去寻找我上面描述的不那么冒险的女性。）
女：其他人有这种感觉吗？男人：如果一个女人看起来很忙/满足，就像她不需要你一样，你会继续前进吗？你如何过你最好的单身生活（这本质上涉及满足你自己的需求，让自己忙碌和充实）并且仍然为伴侣留下“空间”？任何基于经验的实用技巧或建议都会有所帮助。还有其他智慧/鼓励的话吗？你什么时候放弃约会/寻找？在什么时候你会让自己变得更传统，以扩大对伴侣的看法，即使这不是你只为自己而生活时会做的事情？ TL;DR：在一段快乐、非常健康的长期关系和一段时间的休息之后，我重新进入约会场景。我觉得我真的很了解自己，对自己很满意，并且知道我想要一个伴侣做什么，所以我认为约会会更容易。但这似乎让事情变得更加困难，因为我看着男人和那些似乎自己并不快乐的女人结对，并告诉我诸如“我钦佩你，但你不需要我”或“你的生活”之类的话。里面没有其他人的空间。”其他人实际上如何平衡过他们想要的单身生活（例如，必须自给自足，因为你必须这样做）和为伴侣留出空间？添加，截至 8/4、8/7 编辑：哇！无法相信有用和支持的评论。我现在已经阅读了所有这些。感谢所有在线程被锁定后发表评论或给我发消息的人（以及奖励，天哪！）。我希望其他人发现这个线程和我一样有用。我希望我有时间在线程被锁定之前进行更多互动，单独回复。知道我并不孤单，这感觉很棒。我也很欣赏男士坦率地回应想要感到被需要/需要/优先考虑的反应。每个人对情感脆弱性/联系的评论都非常有帮助；这是我知道我需要做的事情，有一天我可能会在我有时间进一步消化这里所有有用的评论后发表一篇文章。有些人评论说我列出了一堆不会使我成为帖子中有价值的合作伙伴的东西，是的 - 这就是帖子的全部意义！指出当你独自一人时很重要的事情（养活自己、拥有社区、独自寻找快乐）与潜在合作伙伴所看重的并不完全相同（尤其是，也许，在这种情况下女性寻找男性）！最后，老实说，我并不是要贬低帖子中的某一行，我把它留在这里，所以评论是有道理的，因为我不想隐瞒它。我的许多最亲密的朋友——其中大多数都受过同等教育、成功和高收入者——是我描述的那些不接受单身生活的女性，我从未说过（当然也不是暗示）女性以不同的方式度过他们的空闲时间并不聪明或在专业上取得成功。我的意思是，我认识的许多目前处于恋爱关系中的女性并没有优先考虑创造她们自己满意的美好生活，而是公开表示她们正在等待伴侣做 x、y 或 z并在此期间花时间和他们的电视/宠物一起出去玩。意识到基于某人选择的电视台或健身计划的广泛概括没有帮助，我想直接向我冒犯的每个人道歉。
note_en:
Does anyone else feel like the “live your best single life” and “be happy on your own” advice backfires, especially for single women in their 30s?
 Me: 37 female professional in a big city. A few fancy degrees. A stable, high-earning job. Own my own condo. Interesting/quirky hobbies (mountaineering, cycling, photography). Travel a lot and to weird places, often alone (did Peace Corps after college, so I’m very comfortable being solo in foreign countries). Have friends from different stages of life. Did several years of therapy and so I like to think I have at least have some self-awareness. (Because people will ask, I do not have trouble matching/getting dates, so I don’t think my appearance is the sole reason I’m still single. And I use recent pictures.)
 I have pretty much the life I always wanted, except for a partner. And I’ve worked hard at building it. But I feel that the more successful and interesting my life is, the less men seem interested in me as a long-term partner. And the fewer men I meet could add to my life in a meaningful way.
 But just because I’m relatively happy alone and do not “need” a partner / go and do everything I want to do without waiting for someone to go with me (travel, buying a house, etc.), it doesn’t mean I don’t want to fall in love any less than the next person. I’d love to have someone to share the ups and downs with; to explore the world with; to be a teammate and build a life with; oh and fulfilling sex on a regular basis is pretty darn nice too.
 However, it seems that the more I “work on myself” to make my current life great, the more it estranges me from potential partners. I’ve had men tell me that I’m easy to walk away from because I don’t “need” them. I’ve had men say that my life feels too complete/full to have a partner — like there’s no place for them in it.
 But to me, needing someone and waiting on someone are just signs that you aren’t living a happy/healthy single life. I feel like all the things I do to make myself happy, healthy, and successful on my own (get that promotion at work; go to Mexico to climb some volcanos; visit my college friends who live in different cities; sign up for a 100-mile bike ride) are actively hindering my dating prospects because Joe or Sam or Pablo doesn’t value those things at all. Instead, I see the men that say that they want women like me actually picking the ones that spend more time on their appearance (hair, markup, fashion) and more time watching Bravo, doing yoga, cuddling with their goldendoodle. These women are waiting on a guy to travel or do anything by themselves, and are often honest about that.
 (And before people tell me I will meet someone when I least expect it if I just live my life: Well I do that but I haven’t. I don’t often met single, attractive men through travel or hobbies. Most men I come across are partnered. The single ones end up thinking of me as an adrenaline buddy and going for the less adventurous women I describe above.)
 Women: Does anyone else feel like this? Men: Is there truth to the fact that if a woman seems busy/content, like she doesn’t need you, you’ll move on?
 How do you live your best single life (which inherently involves meeting your own needs, keeping yourself busy and fulfilled) and still leave “space” for a partner? Any practical tips or advice based on experience would be helpful.
 Any other words of wisdom/encouragement? At what point do you give up dating/looking? At what point do you make yourself more conventional to widen your shot at a partner, even if it’s not what you’d do if you were living your life just for yourself?
 TL;DR: I reentered the dating scene after a happy, pretty healthy long-term relationship and some time off. And I felt like I really know myself, am content with myself, and know what I want in a partner so I thought dating would be easier. But it’s just seemed to make it more difficult as I watch men pair with women who don’t seem to be as happy on their own and tell me things like “I admire you, but you don’t need me” or “your life doesn’t have space for anyone else in it.” How do other people practically balance living the single life they would like to (eg, necessarily being self-sufficient because you have to be) and leaving space for a partner?
  Addition, edited as of 8/4, 8/7: Wow! Cannot believe the helpful and supportive comments. I’ve now read all of them. Thank you to everyone who’s commented or who messaged me after the thread was locked (and for the awards, gosh!). I hope others find this thread as useful as I have. I wish I’d had the time to interact more, respond individually, before the thread was locked.
 It feels amazing know I’m not alone. I also appreciate the candidness of the men’s responses about wanting to feel wanted/needed/prioritized. And everyone’s comments on emotional vulnerability/connection were incredibly helpful; it’s something I know I need to work on and I might do a post on that one day after I have time to further digest all the helpful comments here. Some people commented that I’m listing a bunch of things that don’t make me a valuable partner in the post, and well yes—that was the whole point of the post! To point out that the things that are important when you’re on your own (supporting yourself, having community, finding joy on your own) aren’t really the same things that are valued by potential partners (especially, perhaps, in the case of women seeking men)!!
 Finally, I honestly didn’t mean to be degrading with one of the lines in the post, which I’ve left in here so the comments make sense and because I don’t want to hide from it. Many of my closest friends — most of whom are equally educated, successful, and high earners — are the women I described as not embracing their single life and I didn’t ever say (and certainly didn’t mean to imply) that women that spend their free time differently aren’t smart or professionally successful. The point I intended was that many of the women I know that are currently in relationships didn’t prioritize creating an awesome life they were happy with on their own and instead openly said they were waiting for a partner to do x, y, or z and just spent time hanging out with their TV/pet in the meantime. Realize that broad generalizations based on someone’s choice of tv station or fitness routine aren’t helpful, and wanted to directly apologize to everyone I offended.
title: 什么时候过“最好的单身生活”会适得其反？
title_en: When does living your “best single life” backfire?
